item.title: Geopolitics and Tech Woes Put Traders on Edge Before Weekend
item.author: Alexander Nicholson
item.content:encoded: Even as US stock futures point to tentative gains on Wall Street, AI spending plans and Middle East tensions give plenty to worry about

item.title: Dollar Set for Best Week in a Month as Haven Flows Offer Support
item.author: Vassilis Karamanis
item.content:encoded: The dollar is heading for its best week in a month as investors return to the traditional haven amid heightened geopolitical tensions.

item.title: Gulf States Set for Debt Binge to Build Routes Avoiding Hormuz
item.author: Abhinav Ramnarayan
item.content:encoded: Traders are preparing for a spike in borrowing from Persian Gulf nations as the region’s oil exporters seek financing to build costly bypasses around the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Euronext Mulls Setting Up a Private Market to Rival the UK’s
item.author: Charles Capel, Pablo Mayo Cerqueiro and Laura Noonan
item.content:encoded: Euronext NV is considering creating a market for private securities, potentially ramping up competition with the London Stock Exchange Group Plc as bourses seek to offer investors access to fast-growing private companies.

item.title: BofA’s Raedler Says Investors Are Ignoring Stock-Market Risks
item.author: Matt Clinch and Anna Edwards
item.content:encoded: Stock markets are pricing in a scenario where everything goes right, leaving investors uncompensated for mounting risks, according to Sebastian Raedler, head of European equity strategy at Bank of America Corp.

item.title: Mauritius Tax Wave Threatens to Erode Its Banks’ Profitability
item.author: Adelaide Changole and Kamlesh Bhuckory
item.content:encoded: A raft of new corporate taxes that could erode banks’ profitability is testing Mauritius’s long-standing status as a low-tax gateway for cross-border capital, according to Moody’s Ratings.

item.title: PIC: Why South Africa’s Biggest Fund Manager Is in Turmoil
item.author: Alexander Parker and Mike Cohen
item.content:encoded: The South African firm that oversees state workers’ pensions has been plunged into a fresh crisis triggered by the suspension of its chief executive and the resignation of its chairman.

item.title: Victoria Plc Slumps to £326 Million Loss as Debt Deal Bites
item.author: Tuhin Kar
item.content:encoded: Victoria Plc reported a wider annual loss as the troubled British carpetmaker counted the cost of its recent debt refinancing.

item.title: Zara’s Ortega Buys Paris Office Complex for About €800 Million
item.author: Clara Hernanz Lizarraga
item.content:encoded: Zara billionaire Amancio Ortega bought a business center in Paris from Invesco Ltd., in what is his biggest transaction in Europe.

item.title: How Franchise Restaurants Opened the Door to the Gig Economy
item.author: Joe Weisenthal and Tracy Alloway
item.content:encoded: The ruthless labor history of your favorite fast food joint.

item.title: Odd Lots: How Franchising Led to the Gig Economy (Podcast)
item.author: Joe Weisenthal and Tracy Alloway
item.content:encoded: When the fast food industry began booming in the 1950s, it did so via a new business model known as the franchise. This model allowed independent operators to license trademarks from a business like McDonald’s or Dunkin Donuts, and it soon spread across the country, with huge consequences for how Americans are employed. Legal battles fought by franchises eventually opened the door to what’s now known as the gig economy, allowing Uber drivers to be treated in much the same way as the operator of

item.title: Oil falls back below $100 as Trump weighs ‘massive attack’
item.content:encoded: Middle East tensions have risen after Red Sea attacks by Houthi militants threatened to squeeze global supplies further

item.title: Russian online retailer Wildberries becomes target for Ukraine’s drones
item.content:encoded: Strikes on Wildberries have killed nine people and left small sellers facing millions of roubles in losses

item.title: US hits 60 countries with new duties as Trump rebuilds tariff wall
item.content:encoded: Washington moves to reimpose 10 per cent duties after previous blanket levies were struck down by Supreme Court

item.title: Tanker Exits Red Sea Dark as China Ship Heads Toward Strait
item.author: Weilun Soon
item.content:encoded: A Greek tanker carrying Saudi crude exited the Red Sea with its transponder off, while another Chinese vessel and Russian cargoes headed toward the Bab el-Mandeb strait, after Houthi attacks heightened risks.

item.title: Dangote Prefers London Over Dubai for Listing of Cement Unit
item.author: Jennifer Zabasajja
item.content:encoded: Dangote Cement Plc prefers to list its cement business in London because it will be quicker to sell shares on the exchange in the UK than in Dubai, according to Mariya Dangote, a board member in the cement business.

item.title: Global Bonds Are Reeling as Oil Surge Renews Inflation Threat
item.author: Greg Ritchie and Cameron Fozi
item.content:encoded: Global bonds are being pummeled by the latest resurgence in energy prices, delivering losses to investors who bet the worst of this year’s rout was over and teeing up credibility tests for central bankers.

item.title: Welcome to the era of financial candyfloss
item.content:encoded: The value of real assets in the US is shrinking as financialisation soars

item.title: The field marshal’s gambit
item.content:encoded: Asim Munir is trying to refashion Pakistan’s place in the world — and bolster his power at home

item.title: South Korea’s cash-rich winners of AI boom go on US buying spree
item.content:encoded: Companies make biggest US investment push in years to build up tech capabilities and avoid Donald Trump’s tariffs

item.title: Trump to Take Up Manila’s Concerns With Xi, Philippines Says
item.author: Ditas B Lopez
item.content:encoded: US President Donald Trump assured Philippine leader Ferdinand Marcos Jr. that he would take up Manila’s concerns when he meets with Chinese President Xi Jinping, the Southeast Asian nation said.

item.title: Modi Seeks to Calm Anger as India Student Protests Widen
item.author: Ruchi Bhatia and Aryan Gupta
item.content:encoded: Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi made a fresh bid to calm mounting anger over examination paper leaks as students continued nationwide protests on Friday, with demands growing for the education minister to step down.

item.title: Intel posts fastest growth in 15 years as AI data centres demand fuels sales
item.content:encoded: Chipmaker’s revenue jumped 25% in the second quarter

item.title: Goldman, BofA See 2024 Flashback in China Before Politburo Meets
item.author: Bloomberg News
item.content:encoded: As the calendar turned to July, Chinese retail sales were decelerating fast, the stock market was in the doldrums and investment unexpectedly turned for the worse. Public spending shrank as local governments struggled with financing.

item.title: Japan Is Whipsawing Wall Street’s CLO Machine, Study Shows
item.author: Scott Carpenter
item.content:encoded: The $1.3 trillion US CLO market is taking its cues from Tokyo as much as Wall Street.

item.title: Stocks Get Some Relief as Brent Slips Below $100: Markets Wrap
item.author: Andre Janse van Vuuren and Sagarika Jaisinghani
item.content:encoded: US stocks are finding some respite after posting their biggest drop this month as Brent crude eased below $100 a barrel and bond yields halted their ascent.

item.title: Latest Oil Market News and Analysis for July 24
item.author: Grant Smith and Paul Burkhardt
item.content:encoded: Brent crude retreated after hitting $100 a barrel in the previous session on concern over widening risks to Middle East supplies, as US President Donald Trump’s revived trade tariffs posed economic headwinds.

item.title: Trump’s dubious nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia
item.content:encoded: An accord lacking ‘gold standard’ safeguards on enrichment would carry proliferation risks

item.title: The world’s balance-sheet is out of kilter with its economy
item.content:encoded: Restoring balance could be painful

item.title: A brief history of Luddism
item.content:encoded: States ultimately decide how fast technology is adopted

item.title: Can China dominate AI exports, too?
item.content:encoded: Robotaxis offer an early test case

item.title: The rise of the deserving rich
item.content:encoded: Today’s billionaires are more likely than yesterday’s to have made their own money

item.title: Can prediction markets win over Wall Street?
item.content:encoded: Clearing the regulatory hurdles is only half the battle

item.title: How investors learned to live with inflation
item.content:encoded: Fewer than ever believe central bankers will bring it back to target

item.title: How to shrink the Fed’s $7trn balance-sheet
item.content:encoded: Kevin Warsh will struggle to reverse the effects of bond-buying

item.title: Can Kevin Warsh’s Fed force 5 reimagine monetary policymaking?
item.content:encoded: The new chairman enlists heavy-hitters to fight a handful of gnarly problems

item.title: China’s trade gap is narrowing. And other surprises
item.content:encoded: The world’s second-biggest economy is stumbling into fiscal austerity

item.title: America’s Hormuz brinkmanship is worsening a global fuel crunch
item.content:encoded: Rising oil prices are only part of the problem

item.title: What investment gurus get wrong
item.content:encoded: To see a country’s financial follies, look to its celebrity advisers

item.title: The stubborn scarcity of female breadwinners
item.content:encoded: Wives who out-earn their husbands remain rare in America

item.title: Storm clouds gather over America’s financial supremacy
item.content:encoded: Its payments firms may be the first casualties

item.title: China may struggle to fund Xi Jinping’s tech dreams
item.content:encoded: Even though capital markets are staging a comeback

item.title: An ageing society might not cost too much
item.content:encoded: A dystopian vision of the future may not come to pass

item.title: Three ways the LNG market could crack before winter
item.content:encoded: War, weather and outages may still send gas prices soaring

item.title: AI has taken over the stock market. The bond market is next
item.content:encoded: Judging credit risk of the AI boom is difficult

item.title: Europe’s economy is a mess. Its stock markets are a steal
item.content:encoded: International investors still aren’t interested

item.title: The World Bank has ditched its climate targets
item.content:encoded: The rise and fall of the institution’s love for green growth

item.title: Women’s progress at work is stalling
item.content:encoded: Is the “Lean In” generation leaning out?

item.title: Is The Economist always wrong?
item.content:encoded: We used artificial intelligence to test the accuracy of our forecasts

item.title: How much will a 4th of July cookout cost this year?
item.content:encoded: We calculate the rising price of Americans’ summer staples
